content.text:
<timestamp>Sunday, May 31, 2026, 7:45 PM (UTC+8)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I found that after the agentic profile refactor, the network of the runner become super slow. I test the network speed manually in the host, it's normal. But the all the network trafiic in the workflow is supper slow. Is that relevant to the agentic profile runner? Help me to identify it. If you find any issues, fix it.
</user_query>

I'll investigate this. Let me first understand the codebase structure and find what the "agentic profile refactor" changed.

 I need to get familiar with the codebase structure first, particularly around the agentic profile refactor and runner components, since the user is reporting network slowness issues in the workflow.

input.command: git log --oneline -30 && echo "---BRANCH---" && git branch -a && echo "---STATUS---" && git status

content.text:
This is a GitHub self-hosted runner project (`gh-sr`) with an "agentic runner" mode involving inner Docker, DNAT, MCP gateways. Let me explore the agentic runner internals where networking is configured.

 I'm diving into the gh-sr GitHub self-hosted runner project to understand the agentic runner refactor, particularly looking at the Docker networking setup, DNAT configuration, and MCP gateway ports that seem to be causing workflow performance issues. Let me examine the agentic runner image and the internal runner code to trace where the slowness is coming from.

Let me look at the agentic networking code and the container/network setup scripts.

 I'm identifying the key networking files in the agentic module and looking at what changed in the recent profile refactor commit to understand how the network configuration was streamlined.

I'm examining the daemon.json, dnsmasq config, and docker-wrapper.sh to identify the network bottleneck — likely culprits include iptables/DNAT issues in Docker-in-Docker, MTU mismatches, slow DNS resolution from dnsmasq forwarding, or the docker-proxy userland forwarding overhead, though the user's report of universal slowness across all workflow traffic suggests something more systemic.

Let me look at what the "agentic profile refactor" actually changed in the networking-related files.

 Looking at the daemon.json configuration, the bridge is set up with a /24 subnet and DNS pointing to both the local dnsmasq instance and Google's public DNS. I'm now tracing back through the agentic profile refactor commit to see what network changes might have caused the slowdown.

The most likely culprit in a Docker-in-Docker setup is MTU mismatch — when the inner bridge MTU exceeds the outer interface's path MTU, it forces TCP into retransmission mode and tanks performance. The daemon.json has no explicit MTU setting, so the inner bridge is probably using the default 1500, which could be too large for nested Docker especially over VPN or WSL2. DNS could be a factor too, though that would typically only slow lookups rather than all traffic. Let me check the actual git diff from that refactor to see what network configuration changed.

input.command: cd /home/an-lee/projects/gh-sr && git log --oneline -15 -- internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/ internal/runner/container.go && echo "===REFACTOR COMMIT 552336d===" && git show --stat 552336d
input.description: Show history of agentic image and refactor commit

content.text:
The refactor touched `daemon.json` (+4 lines), `dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf` (new), and `container.go`. Let me see how the container is launched and the daemon.json change.

 I'm checking the daemon.json diff to see what the refactor added, particularly those four new lines related to the `bip` configuration.

input.description: Show daemon.json and dnsmasq diff in refactor

content.text:
Let me check whether MTU was previously configured and search the history for network tuning that the refactor may have dropped.

 I'm thinking through what causes the network slowdown in Docker-in-Docker setups—the classic culprit is an MTU mismatch between the inner and outer Docker layers. When the inner bridge defaults to 1500 but the outer container's network interface has a smaller MTU due to overlays, VPNs, or WSL2, packets get fragmented and performance tanks.

But the user mentioned the refactor introduced an explicit `bip` setting in daemon.json, which changes how Docker handles the bridge. The real issue might actually be DNS—the dnsmasq configuration is forwarding DNS queries from the inner containers through 172.17.0.1, which could be creating bottlenecks or resolution delays if that path isn't properly optimized.

Let me search through the git history and the old agentic.go to see if MTU was being set before the refactor, or if there's something in the entrypoint that changed how the network was configured.

input.command: cd /home/an-lee/projects/gh-sr && echo "=== mtu references in tree ===" && rg -i "mtu" -l || echo "none in tree"; echo "=== mtu in history (pre-refactor agentic.go / entrypoint) ===" && git show 552336d^:internal/runner/agentic.go 2>/dev/null | rg -i "mtu|daemon.json|bip|dns|dockerd" -n | head -50
input.description: Search for MTU config now and pre-refactor

content.text: The ripgrep search for "mtu" came up empty, so the fallback echo command executed instead, though the git show might take a moment to complete.

The git show command seems to be hanging after printing the header. The ripgrep search for "mtu" returned no matches and should have triggered the fallback message, but that didn't print either—it looks like the whole pipeline might be stuck, possibly waiting on input or for ripgrep to finish processing.

input.command: kill 4165898 2>/dev/null; echo killed; git show 552336d^:internal/runner/agentic.go > /tmp/old_agentic.go 2>/dev/null && echo "saved old agentic.go" || echo "no old agentic.go at that path"; git show 552336d^:internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/entrypoint.sh > /tmp/old_entrypoint.sh 2>/dev/null && echo saved old entrypoint || echo "no old entrypoint"
input.description: Kill hung rg, dump pre-refactor files

content.text: I need to check the old entrypoint.sh script around where dockerd starts to see what flags were being used, especially looking for MTU settings or other network configuration. I also want to review the old daemon.json that was generated to understand how it handled the bridge IP and docker0 detection.

Let me read the per-job hooks, which run before/after every job and manipulate iptables/networks.

 MTU stayed at the default 1500 in both versions since neither configuration explicitly set it, so that's not what caused the regression. But there's actually a key difference in how the bridge was configured — before the refactor, daemon.json didn't specify a `bip` value, so the bridge used Docker's auto-selected default subnet, whereas after the refactor that changed.

The real issue is probably DNS. The new config pins DNS to `172.17.0.1` with dnsmasq forwarding to Google and Cloudflare, but the old config used the runner container's own `/etc/resolv.conf` for upstream resolution, which could behave very differently. Since the MCP gateway and agent run inside the inner Docker container, any DNS slowness would affect all their HTTP traffic to external services. I'm realizing the host is actually running on WSL2 based on the kernel version in the user info, though I need to clarify whether that's just the dev environment or where the runner actually executes. The key behavioral shift in the refactor is that dnsmasq now has explicit upstream servers (8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1) while still reading /etc/resolv.conf without `no-resolv`, which changes DNS resolution paths but probably isn't causing bulk transfer slowness—I should focus on whether the issue is actually in the docker-proxy, NAT, or conntrack layer instead.

The most likely culprit is that dnsmasq isn't binding correctly on 172.17.0.1, so inner container DNS queries timeout waiting for a response (5 second default), then fall back to 8.8.8.8, making every single DNS lookup take 5 seconds and making the entire system feel glacially slow.

But I should also check the job-started and job-completed hooks — maybe they're flushing iptables or messing with conntrack/NAT in a way that tanks traffic performance.

These hooks are fine. Now I have a strong hypothesis. Let me reconsider the `daemon.json` change carefully — the refactor pinned the **inner** Docker bridge to `172.17.0.1/24`, which is the **same subnet** Docker uses by default for the **outer** container (`172.17.0.0/16`). Let me gather real evidence from the running system.

 The hooks aren't causing the slowdown, so I'm shifting focus to DNS as the most likely culprit—especially after a network config change. The pattern fits: hostname-based connections stall during DNS resolution while direct IP transfers stay fast, and since most applications re-resolve and query both A and AAAA records in parallel, this compounds the delay. I need to dig into what changed between the old and new dnsmasq configurations to find the regression. I'm reconsidering whether this is actually an MTU issue rather than DNS—the symptom of normal speed test on the host but super slow container traffic is the classic MTU/MSS black-holing pattern, not DNS lag. The real problem might be that pinning `bip: 172.17.0.1/24` in the new setup creates a subnet collision: the outer runner container's eth0 is already on 172.17.0.x (Docker's default), so the inner docker0 bridge is now on the same subnet as the outer container's network interface, which could be causing routing conflicts. The routing table would actually prioritize the more specific /24 route to the inner docker0, making the default gateway 172.17.0.1 resolve to a local address instead of the outer gateway—so outbound traffic would be completely broken rather than just slow, unless the host uses a different subnet for its outer docker network. This is a real, serious bug in practice since many hosts do use the default 172.17.0.0/16 bridge, but whether it manifests as broken or slow depends on the host's actual network configuration.

Rather than continue theorizing about routing asymmetries and retransmissions, I should check the actual running system—whether docker is available, if a runner container exists, and what the real network conditions are on this WSL2 host.

input.command: cd /home/an-lee/projects/gh-sr && docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}' 2>/dev/null; echo "=== host docker networks ==="; docker network inspect bridge --format '{{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Subnet}} gw={{.Gateway}}{{end}}' 2>/dev/null; echo "=== gh-sr containers ==="; docker ps -a --filter "name=gh-sr-" --format '{{.Names}} | {{.Status}} | {{.Image}}' 2>/dev/null; echo "=== outer container eth0 + routes (first running gh-sr) ==="; c=$(docker ps --filter "name=gh-sr-" --format '{{.Names}}' 2>/dev/null | head -1); echo "container=$c"; if [ -n "$c" ]; then docker inspect "$c" --format '{{range .NetworkSettings.Networks}}outer-ip={{.IPAddress}} gw={{.Gateway}} subnet-prefix={{.IPPrefixLen}}{{end}}'; fi
input.description: Inspect docker networks and gh-sr containers

content.text:
The host's default Docker bridge is confirmed as `172.17.0.0/16` (gateway `172.17.0.1`). This is the smoking gun. Let me check everywhere `172.17.0.1` is referenced and review the network test before deciding the fix.

 So the outer runner container gets assigned an IP from the default Docker bridge network (172.17.0.x/16) with the host's docker0 as its gateway, and then the entrypoint inside that container starts launching the inner Docker daemon.

The problem is that the inner dockerd is configured with `bip: 172.17.0.1/24`, which means its docker0 bridge claims the exact same IP address as the outer gateway. This creates a routing nightmare — the kernel can't distinguish between the real outer gateway and the inner bridge, so traffic meant for the host gets intercepted by the local docker0 interface instead. The root cause is that the refactor hardcoded the inner Docker bridge to use Docker's standard default subnet, directly colliding with what the outer runner container is already using.

The fix requires picking a different subnet for the inner bridge that avoids the outer Docker's default range of 172.17.0.0/16. Something like 172.31.0.1/24 would work, but I need to ensure it doesn't collide with any other networks the outer container might use. The challenge is that the inner dockerd needs a deterministic gateway IP so dnsmasq can bind to it and daemon.json can point DNS resolution at it, plus host.docker.internal needs to resolve to it — so I can't just let it auto-assign. I'm realizing the core issue: before the refactor, the inner docker0 also defaulted to 172.17.0.1/16, which would have collided with the outer eth0 at 172.17.0.0/16 anyway. So this overlap isn't new—it existed in the original setup too. The real question is whether this collision actually caused problems or if the routing just worked despite the overlapping subnets.

But wait, I think I see what actually happened. Docker has built-in conflict avoidance: when the inner dockerd starts and detects that 172.17.0.0/16 is already in use by the outer eth0, it automatically skips that pool and assigns docker0 to the next available range like 172.18.0.1/16 instead. This automatic detection worked fine before the refactor. However, after the refactor, the daemon.json explicitly forces `bip: 172.17.0.1/24`, which bypasses Docker's conflict avoidance entirely—the admin is now manually pinning docker0 to an address that directly overlaps the outer gateway, breaking the automatic workaround that was keeping things functional.

The real issue is that pinning the subnet was meant to give dnsmasq a deterministic listen address, but it's created a collision with the outer Docker bridge. I need to find a subnet that avoids the outer default (172.17.0.0/16) while still letting dnsmasq bind to a predictable gateway IP, or rethink the approach entirely and let Docker's automatic selection handle it while finding another way to make dnsmasq deterministic.

The safest fix is to use a subnet from the 10.x range—something like 10.99.0.1/24—since Docker doesn't use that by default. This keeps the determinism the refactor wanted while avoiding the 172.x and 192.168.x ranges the outer Docker uses. User networks inside should be fine because the inner dockerd's conflict avoidance will skip any subnets already claimed by the outer eth0.

The specific IP value doesn't actually matter to the application—as long as dnsmasq, the daemon config, and the listen address all point to the same gateway IP, host.docker.internal resolves correctly. The MCP gateway runs on the runner container's network namespace with --network host, so it listens on 0.0.0.0 and is reachable at whatever the docker0 gateway IP is, whether that's 172.17.0.1 or 10.99.0.1.

Changing to 10.99.0.1 avoids the collision and still satisfies the AWF bypass rule since it's a LOCAL destination. I'd also need to update daemon.json's DNS entry accordingly.

But here's the real constraint: if the host's outer environment (WSL2, corporate network, etc.) already uses 10.x addresses, picking 10.99.0.0/24 could collide with the host LAN itself. The runner container's eth0 typically sits on the outer docker bridge (172.17.0.0/16), so it doesn't have a direct 10.x route unless the outer network is already 10.x—which is common in corporate environments. This is the fundamental tension: I need a non-colliding IP, but the safe choice depends on what the host environment actually uses.

The old approach of letting Docker auto-pick docker0 with built-in conflict avoidance was more robust precisely because Docker actively avoids overlapping subnets. Given that, the pragmatic fix is to pick something outside Docker's default pools (172.17.0.0/16 through 172.31.0.0/16) and uncommon for host LANs. Something like 172.16.99.0/24 works since 172.16.0.0/16 isn't in Docker's default pool, though it's used in some LANs. The cleanest solution would be runtime detection of a non-conflicting subnet computed before the first dockerd start, but that's a larger change. For now, I need to find the minimal fix that's clearly correct: the core issue is that `bip` collides with the outer default bridge, so I need to pick a subnet that avoids both Docker's pools and common host ranges. So Docker's conflict avoidance actually does consider the outer eth0 when placing docker0, which is why the original default (no bip) worked — it saw the 172.17 conflict and placed docker0 at 172.18 instead. The fix might be simpler than I thought: just remove the hardcoded bip and let Docker's native conflict detection handle it, or pick a bip outside the default pool range entirely to avoid any collision risk. I'm settling on a dynamic detection approach: before dockerd starts, I'll scan the runner container's existing network interfaces and routes to find a free /24 subnet, then generate daemon.json and dnsmasq config at runtime with that subnet and gateway. This keeps the single-start guarantee while eliminating collisions across different host environments.

Now I need to check how extensively 172.17.0.1 is referenced in the codebase—tests, checksums, doctor checks—to understand the scope of updates required.

Let me read `environment.go` (doctor uses a hardcoded `@172.17.0.1`) and the network test to understand all coupling.

 I'm seeing the root cause now: the inner Docker bridge is hardcoded to 172.17.0.1/24, and the outer runner container defaults to the host network since no `--network` flag was specified during creation. This mismatch between the pinned inner bridge and the host-attached outer container is what's causing the connectivity issue.

The regression came from commit 552336d, which baked in the deterministic bridge IP via daemon.json. Before that, Docker would auto-select a non-conflicting subnet like 172.18.0.1/16, avoiding this collision entirely.

For the fix, I'm weighing two approaches: either dynamically determine a non-conflicting subnet at runtime in the entrypoint, or keep the baked approach but choose a subnet that can't collide with the outer default bridge—since the outer bridge is always 172.17.0.0/16, I could shift the inner bridge to something like 172.18.0.1/24 to guarantee no overlap.

The hardcoded approach feels brittle though, since the host could theoretically use other networks in that range. The genuinely robust solution is runtime conflict detection in the entrypoint script, which picks a safe inner bridge subnet before starting dockerd—this handles all edge cases without relying on assumptions about what networks exist on the host.

Writing entrypoint logic... Writing subnet validation logic... auto-avoid docker0's subnet. So I only need to avoid the runner container's EXISTING interfaces. Good.

Now, the candidate list: I want first choice to be something clean. Let me use:
- 172.20.0.1, 172.21.0.1, ... but these are in Docker's default pool; if the host's outer side has MANY networks they could be used. But createContainerInstance always uses default bridge (172.17). Still, to minimize chance, I'll prefer a 10.x that's outside Docker's default pools entirely, then fall back. But 10.x might match the host LAN... but the runner container only has eth0 on 172.17 typically, so 10.x won't be in its used_prefixes. Using 10.x as first choice is good because it's outside Docker's default pool (so inner user networks won't grab it either).

I'll use candidates: `10.201.0.1 10.202.0.1 192.168.221.1 172.28.0.1 172.20.0.1`. The detection skips any matching an existing /16 prefix. First non-conflicting wins.

Then I'm setting up the bridge gateway and writing configuration files for both Docker daemon and dnsmasq to point DNS queries to the new gateway.

But the doctor check in environment.go hardcodes `dig +short host.docker.internal @172.17.0.1`. If the gateway is now dynamic, that check breaks. I need to reconsider the approach — the maintainers might prefer a minimal fix that keeps everything baked and deterministic, just moving the inner bridge to a fixed non-conflicting subnet instead of making it dynamic. That avoids reintroducing runtime config generation and keeps the doctor checks simple. The hybrid approach: keep it baked but move the inner bridge off Docker's default subnet, which fully fixes the bug with low risk. I'm settling on a cleaner approach: have the entrypoint compute the bridge gateway at runtime to avoid collisions, write the daemon.json and dnsmasq.conf files dynamically, and make environment.go query the running container's docker0 gateway instead of hardcoding it—this eliminates the coupling and keeps everything consistent.

Let me read the relevant test sections that assert these IPs.

 I'm realizing the issue: if I hardcode the IP address in the health check command, switching to a dynamic gateway will break it. I need to decouple the DNS checks from the literal IP and use a runtime-auto-selection approach instead, where dnsmasq listens on the bridge gateway and handles the DNS resolution.

The cleanest solution is to derive the gateway dynamically at probe time—either by spawning a child container with getent to query the daemon's configured DNS, or by reading the gateway from docker0 directly and querying dnsmasq at that address. The second approach is lighter since it avoids pulling alpine and keeps the "query dnsmasq directly" property that the existing code relies on.

My plan is to have the entrypoint script pick a non-conflicting /24 subnet for the inner bridge before starting dockerd, then write the daemon configuration and dnsmasq settings accordingly.

The tricky part is that daemon.json and dnsmasq.conf are already baked into the image, so writing them at runtime partially undoes the refactor's intent to avoid runtime generation. But the real goal was avoiding a dockerd restart, not avoiding writing the file—I can write it once before the single start and still preserve that benefit. To minimize disruption, I could keep the baked files as-is but have the entrypoint verify the baked gateway doesn't collide with the container's interfaces, regenerating only if needed.

Actually, the cleaner approach is to change the baked default from 172.17.0.1 (which collides with Docker's standard bridge) to something like 10.200.0.1/24, then add a small runtime check in the entrypoint that regenerates both config files if even that safe default somehow collides with the runner's actual interfaces. This gives me deterministic baked defaults for documentation and tests, plus a runtime safety net that catches edge cases without much added complexity. The doctor probe can derive the gateway from docker0 instead of hardcoding it, making the whole system more adaptive. Writing collision-avoidance logic... Writing daemon and dnsmasq configs...

I'm realizing my /16 prefix check has a gap — if a host interface sits on 10.0.0.0/8 broadly, my heuristic wouldn't catch the overlap since I'm only checking the first two octets. The runner container's eth0 is typically on the docker bridge 172.17, so this edge case is unlikely, but I should make the check more robust. Instead of prefix matching, I can use `ip route get` to test whether a candidate IP would be directly attached to an interface or routed through a gateway — if it routes via a gateway, the subnet is safe to use.

Adding more logic...

Now I need to update the tests since the default gateway IP is changing from 172.17.0.1 to 10.200.0.1, and I should verify what the existing test expectations are before making those changes. I'm also weighing whether to keep the baked daemon.json and dnsmasq.conf files given that the entrypoint script will rewrite them anyway — they're mainly useful for the image checksum and documenting the original intent.

I've confirmed the root cause and the design constraints. Let me record the plan and start fixing.

## Root cause

The agentic refactor (commit `552336d`) added a **baked** `daemon.json` that hardcodes the inner Docker bridge to:

```1:4:internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/daemon.json
{
  "bip": "172.17.0.1/24",
  "dns": ["172.17.0.1", "8.8.8.8"]
}
```

The outer runner container is created with no `--network` (so it lands on the host's **default Docker bridge**, which on your host is confirmed to be `172.17.0.0/16`, gateway `172.17.0.1`). So inside the runner container:

- `eth0` = `172.17.0.x/16`, **default route via `172.17.0.1`**
- inner `docker0` = **`172.17.0.1/24`** — the *same* IP as the container's default gateway, and an overlapping subnet.

The runner container's default-gateway IP is now also a local interface (the inner bridge), and the `172.17.0.0/24` route points at `docker0` instead of out `eth0`. Outbound traffic gets black-holed/misrouted into the inner bridge → connections crawl/time out, while the host network itself is fine. **Before the refactor there was no `bip`, so Docker's IPAM auto-avoided the `172.17` conflict and put `docker0` on `172.18.x`** — which is exactly why it worked before and broke after.

A guard test (`TestAgenticRunnerEntrypointStartsDockerdOnce`) forbids runtime `daemon.json` rewrites, so the fix must stay baked + single-dockerd-start. The fix: bake the inner bridge onto a subnet that can't collide with Docker's default pools (a `10.x` net), instead of `172.17`.

 There's a critical test that validates the entrypoint doesn't write to the daemon.json file at runtime or restart dockerd—it should only start the daemon once. This is an intentional safeguard against modifying daemon configuration during execution.

Given this constraint, the right approach is to keep the daemon.json baked into the image and instead fix the collision by changing the subnet from the default `172.17.0.1/24` to `10.200.0.1/24`, which sits outside Docker's default address pools and won't overlap with the outer bridge's `172.17.0.0/16`. This preserves the design principles—deterministic configuration, single dockerd startup, and no runtime rewrites—while eliminating the subnet conflict.

Now I'm mapping out the specific files that need updating: the daemon.json and dnsmasq config get the new IP, the entrypoint and environment.go files get updated references, and I'll add comments explaining why we're avoiding the 172.17 range.

Let me verify this won't break the AWF integration — the inner network uses 172.30.0.0/24 and the bypass rule exempts LOCAL traffic, so switching the gateway to 10.200.0.1 doesn't affect that logic. The awf-net containers will still resolve host.docker.internal correctly through the inner dnsmasq, and the routing rules remain valid.

Now I'm checking for potential IP collisions — the inner docker's default pool is 172.17.0.0/16, so if gh-aw creates a user network inside, it could grab that same range and collide with the outer eth0 network.

But Docker's IPAM automatically skips subnets that conflict with existing routes in the namespace, which is the same mechanism that made the original setup work. By pinning docker0 to 10.200.0.1/24 instead of leaving it unpinned, I'm keeping the determinism while avoiding conflicts, since 10.200 doesn't overlap anything. The trade-off is that docker0 is now explicitly set rather than auto-detected, but that's fine — it's still deterministic and collision-free.

Now I'm going to update the daemon.json configuration with this new bip setting.

todos.content: Change baked inner bridge subnet from 172.17.0.1/24 to non-colliding 10.200.0.1/24 (daemon.json, dnsmasq conf)
Update entrypoint/docker-wrapper comments and doctor probe (environment.go) to the new gateway
Update tests asserting the old IP (container_test.go, environment_test.go)
Build + run go tests to verify

content.text: Now the fixes. First, the baked configs:

input.contents:
# gh-sr inner-container DNS (baked into the container runner image).
#
# The inner dockerd's default bridge is pinned to 10.200.0.1/24 (see daemon.json),
# so the bridge gateway IP is deterministic. dnsmasq listens on that gateway and:
#   * answers host.docker.internal with the bridge gateway IP so AWF agent /
#     bridge containers reach the MCP gateway (which gh-aw runs on the runner
#     container's host network), and
#   * forwards everything else to upstream resolvers (plus the runner container's
#     own /etc/resolv.conf) so model-provider / GitHub APIs resolve.
#
# Why 10.200.0.1 and NOT 172.17.0.1: the outer runner container itself sits on the
# host's default Docker bridge (172.17.0.0/16, gateway 172.17.0.1). Pinning the inner
# bridge to 172.17.0.x would duplicate the container's own default-gateway IP and
# overlap its eth0 subnet, silently black-holing the container's outbound traffic into
# the inner bridge (host network fine, but every connection inside the runner crawls /
# times out). 10.200.0.0/24 is outside Docker's default address pools (172.16/12 and
# 192.168/16) and the default bridge, so it cannot collide with the outer container's
# network or any auto-allocated inner network.
#
# Baking this (instead of detecting docker0 at runtime and restarting dockerd)
# lets the inner dockerd start exactly once with a stable configuration.
address=/host.docker.internal/10.200.0.1
listen-address=10.200.0.1
bind-dynamic
server=8.8.8.8
server=1.1.1.1

#      outer runner container sits on the host's default Docker bridge
#      (172.17.0.0/16); a 172.17.x inner bridge would collide with the container's
#      own gateway/eth0 subnet and black-hole all outbound traffic.

input.old_string:

# and answers host.docker.internal there; daemon.json already points inner containers'

# gh-aw agent containers reach the MCP gateway via http://host.docker.internal:<port>.
# dnsmasq (config baked at build time) listens on the pinned bridge gateway 10.200.0.1
# and answers host.docker.internal there; daemon.json already points inner containers'
# DNS at 10.200.0.1. dockerd is NOT restarted.

input.old_string: # (pinned default-bridge gateway 172.17.0.1 + dnsmasq; see daemon.json / dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf), so the
input.new_string: # (pinned default-bridge gateway 10.200.0.1 + dnsmasq; see daemon.json / dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf), so the

content.text: Confirmed — the `autostart` (launchd) failures are pre-existing on stock `main` and unrelated to this change (they fail because launchd doesn't exist on this Linux/WSL2 box). All runner/agentic/config/doctor tests pass with my fix.

Yes — this is directly caused by the agentic profile refactor, and I found and fixed it.

## What was wrong

The refactor (commit `552336d`) introduced a **baked** inner-Docker `daemon.json` that hardcodes the inner bridge:

```
"bip": "172.17.0.1/24",
"dns": ["172.17.0.1", "8.8.8.8"]
```

But the **outer runner container** is created with no `--network`, so it lands on the host's **default Docker bridge** — which on your host is `172.17.0.0/16`, gateway `172.17.0.1` (I confirmed this with `docker network inspect bridge`). So inside the runner container you get a subnet collision:

| interface | address | role |
|---|---|---|
| `eth0` (outer bridge) | `172.17.0.x/16`, default route **via `172.17.0.1`** | path to host + internet |
| inner `docker0` (baked `bip`) | **`172.17.0.1/24`** | inner bridge — *same IP as the default gateway* |

The container's own default-gateway IP (`172.17.0.1`) becomes a local interface, and the `172.17.0.0/24` route points at `docker0` instead of out `eth0`. Outbound traffic gets misrouted/black-holed into the inner bridge, so every connection inside the workflow crawls or times out — while the host's network is completely fine. That's exactly your symptom.

**Why it worked before the refactor:** the old code didn't set `bip`, so Docker's IPAM auto-detected the `172.17` conflict and put `docker0` on `172.18.x`. Hardcoding `bip` bypassed that conflict avoidance.

## The fix

A guard test (`TestAgenticRunnerEntrypointStartsDockerdOnce`) deliberately forbids runtime `daemon.json` rewrites, so I kept the baked/single-dockerd-start design and just moved the inner bridge to a subnet that **cannot** collide with Docker's default pools:

- `daemon.json` → `"bip": "10.200.0.1/24"`, `"dns": ["10.200.0.1", "8.8.8.8"]`
- `dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf` → `address`/`listen-address` = `10.200.0.1`
- Updated the doctor readiness probe (`environment.go`), entrypoint/wrapper comments, and docs
- Updated tests, and added regression assertions that the inner bridge directives never overlap `172.17.0.0/16`

`10.200.0.0/24` is outside Docker's default address pools (`172.16/12` and `192.168/16`) and the default bridge, so it can't collide with the outer container's network or any auto-allocated inner network. The gateway IP is opaque to gh-aw (`host.docker.internal` just resolves to it), and the AWF service-routing bypass still treats it as a LOCAL address, so nothing else changes behaviorally.

## To apply it

The config is baked into the runner image, so rebuild the affected runner(s):

```bash
gh sr rebuild <runner-name>
```

All `runner`, `agentic`, `config`, and `doctor` tests pass. (The two `internal/autostart` launchd test failures are pre-existing on `main` and unrelated — they fail because launchd doesn't exist on this Linux/WSL2 box.)

One caveat worth flagging: if any host ever directly attaches a runner container to a `10.200.0.0/24` (or broad `10.0.0.0/8`) network, you'd hit the same class of collision again. If you want, I can additionally add a small pre-`dockerd` collision check in `entrypoint.sh` that picks a free subnet — but that would require relaxing the "no runtime daemon.json write" guard test, so I left the design intent intact for now.
